A historical chronicle, *Hagurozan Nendaiki*, mentions that in 860, Ennin (Jikaku Daishi) subdued two demons and established a shrine called Gongen-do on a dragon-shaped mountain peak, naming the temple Ryūtō-ji. However, this legend likely pertains to another location near the summit, distinct from the current Ryūtō-ji in Warabioka. == History == The earliest record mentioning Warabioka's sub-temples dates back to 1611. By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[sn:Ryūtō-ji]] [[sn:yūtō-ji]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}}

By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Historically, Warabioka was the largest center of Shugendo practice on Mount Chokai, with Ryūtō-ji serving as the head temple (gakuto-ji) of a mountain temple complex comprising 33 sub-temples. Despite the Meiji-era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, Ryūtō-ji remained a Buddhist temple without secularization. == Origins == Ryūtō-ji is situated at the southeastern edge of Yuza Town, near National Route 345, at the foot of Mount Chokai. Among the Shugendo centers on Mount Chokai, Warabioka was the most influential, with its practitioners known as "Daishu" residing on a hill referred to as Uwadara. The temple's tradition claims it was founded in 807 by Jisho Shonin, though historical records are lacking. A historical chronicle, *Hagurozan Nendaiki*, mentions that in 860, Ennin (Jikaku Daishi) subdued two demons and established a shrine called Gongen-do on a dragon-shaped mountain peak, naming the temple Ryūtō-ji. However, this legend likely pertains to another location near the summit, distinct from the current Ryūtō-ji in Warabioka. == History == The earliest record mentioning Warabioka's sub-temples dates back to 1611. By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

A historical chronicle, *Hagurozan Nendaiki*, mentions that in 860, Ennin (Jikaku Daishi) subdued two demons and established a shrine called Gongen-do on a dragon-shaped mountain peak, naming the temple Ryūtō-ji. However, this legend likely pertains to another location near the summit, distinct from the current Ryūtō-ji in Warabioka. == History == The earliest record mentioning Warabioka's sub-temples dates back to 1611. By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

出羽十三仏札所 第七番（薬師如来） |文化財 = [[薬師如来]]、[[地蔵菩薩]]、[[金剛力士]]、[[阿弥陀如来]]、黄檗版[[一切経]]、[[両界曼荼羅]]図、三千仏曼荼羅図 }} '''Ryūtō-ji''' (龍頭寺) is a temple of the Shingon Chizan Sect located in Warabioka, Yuza Town, Akumi District, Yamagata Prefecture. Historically, Warabioka was the largest center of Shugendo practice on Mount Chokai, with Ryūtō-ji serving as the head temple (gakuto-ji) of a mountain temple complex comprising 33 sub-temples. Despite the Meiji-era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, Ryūtō-ji remained a Buddhist temple without secularization. == Origins == Ryūtō-ji is situated at the southeastern edge of Yuza Town, near National Route 345, at the foot of Mount Chokai. Among the Shugendo centers on Mount Chokai, Warabioka was the most influential, with its practitioners known as "Daishu" residing on a hill referred to as Uwadara. The temple's tradition claims it was founded in 807 by Jisho Shonin, though historical records are lacking. A historical chronicle, *Hagurozan Nendaiki*, mentions that in 860, Ennin (Jikaku Daishi) subdued two demons and established a shrine called Gongen-do on a dragon-shaped mountain peak, naming the temple Ryūtō-ji. However, this legend likely pertains to another location near the summit, distinct from the current Ryūtō-ji in Warabioka. == History == The earliest record mentioning Warabioka's sub-temples dates back to 1611. By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryuuzuji.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Yuza|WD=Q1347481}}|coordinates={{Coord|38.996069|N|139.943989|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773}}}} {{日本の寺院 |名称 = 龍頭寺 |画像 = [[File:Ryuuzuji.JPG|250px|龍頭寺]] |所在地 = [[山形県]][[飽海郡]][[遊佐町]]蕨岡字松岡45 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|38|59|45.85|N|139|56|38.36|E|type:landmark_region:JP-06}} |山号 = 鳥海山 |宗旨 = [[真言宗]] |宗派 = [[真言宗智山派]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[薬師如来]]坐像 |創建年 = 伝 大同2年（807年） |開基 = 慈照上人 |中興年 = |中興 = 深海法印 |正式名 = 鳥海山龍頭寺 |別称 = |札所等 = 庄内三十三観音霊場 第十九番
出羽十三仏札所 第七番（薬師如来） |文化財 = [[薬師如来]]、[[地蔵菩薩]]、[[金剛力士]]、[[阿弥陀如来]]、黄檗版[[一切経]]、[[両界曼荼羅]]図、三千仏曼荼羅図 }} '''Ryūtō-ji''' (龍頭寺) is a temple of the Shingon Chizan Sect located in Warabioka, Yuza Town, Akumi District, Yamagata Prefecture. Historically, Warabioka was the largest center of Shugendo practice on Mount Chokai, with Ryūtō-ji serving as the head temple (gakuto-ji) of a mountain temple complex comprising 33 sub-temples. Despite the Meiji-era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, Ryūtō-ji remained a Buddhist temple without secularization. == Origins == Ryūtō-ji is situated at the southeastern edge of Yuza Town, near National Route 345, at the foot of Mount Chokai. Among the Shugendo centers on Mount Chokai, Warabioka was the most influential, with its practitioners known as "Daishu" residing on a hill referred to as Uwadara. The temple's tradition claims it was founded in 807 by Jisho Shonin, though historical records are lacking. A historical chronicle, *Hagurozan Nendaiki*, mentions that in 860, Ennin (Jikaku Daishi) subdued two demons and established a shrine called Gongen-do on a dragon-shaped mountain peak, naming the temple Ryūtō-ji. However, this legend likely pertains to another location near the summit, distinct from the current Ryūtō-ji in Warabioka. == History == The earliest record mentioning Warabioka's sub-temples dates back to 1611. By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Yusa Town]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryuuzuji.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}}|prefecture={{ill|Yuza|WD=Q1347481|1=Yuza}}|coordinates={{Coord|38.996069|N|139.943989|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}}} {{日本の寺院 |名称 = 龍頭寺 |画像 = [[File:Ryuuzuji.JPG|250px|龍頭寺]] |所在地 = [[山形県]][[飽海郡]][[遊佐町]]蕨岡字松岡45 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|38|59|45.85|N|139|56|38.36|E|type:landmark_region:JP-06}} |山号 = 鳥海山 |宗旨 = [[真言宗]] |宗派 = [[真言宗智山派]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[薬師如来]]坐像 |創建年 = 伝 大同2年（807年） |開基 = 慈照上人 |中興年 = |中興 = 深海法印 |正式名 = 鳥海山龍頭寺 |別称 = |札所等 = 庄内三十三観音霊場 第十九番
出羽十三仏札所 第七番（薬師如来） |文化財 = [[薬師如来]]、[[地蔵菩薩]]、[[金剛力士]]、[[阿弥陀如来]]、黄檗版[[一切経]]、[[両界曼荼羅]]図、三千仏曼荼羅図 }} '''Ryūtō-ji''' (龍頭寺) is a temple of the Shingon Chizan Sect located in Warabioka, Yuza Town, Akumi District, Yamagata Prefecture. Historically, Warabioka was the largest center of Shugendo practice on Mount Chokai, with Ryūtō-ji serving as the head temple (gakuto-ji) of a mountain temple complex comprising 33 sub-temples. Despite the Meiji-era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, Ryūtō-ji remained a Buddhist temple without secularization. == Origins == Ryūtō-ji is situated at the southeastern edge of Yuza Town, near National Route 345, at the foot of Mount Chokai. Among the Shugendo centers on Mount Chokai, Warabioka was the most influential, with its practitioners known as "Daishu" residing on a hill referred to as Uwadara. The temple's tradition claims it was founded in 807 by Jisho Shonin, though historical records are lacking. A historical chronicle, *Hagurozan Nendaiki*, mentions that in 860, Ennin (Jikaku Daishi) subdued two demons and established a shrine called Gongen-do on a dragon-shaped mountain peak, naming the temple Ryūtō-ji. However, this legend likely pertains to another location near the summit, distinct from the current Ryūtō-ji in Warabioka. == History == The earliest record mentioning Warabioka's sub-temples dates back to 1611. By then, the organization of 33 sub-temples was firmly established. Ryūtō-ji, originally known as Kannon-ji, transitioned to its current name after 1655. The temple maintained connections with the Shugendo practices of Mount Haguro and later became part of the Shingon Chizan Sect following the Meiji government's abolition of Shugendo in 1872. During the Edo period, Ryūtō-ji was under the direct jurisdiction of Daigo Sanbo-in in Edo, with significant authority in Shugendo rituals. Even after the Meiji Restoration, the temple continued as a Buddhist institution while many associated sub-temples converted to Shinto practices. == Present Day == Today, Ryūtō-ji preserves its historical architecture, including the main hall and the registered tangible cultural property, Oizumi-bo Nagaya-mon. The temple houses statues of Yakushi Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, Kongorikishi, Amida Nyorai, and important mandalas. == Cultural Significance == The temple is part of the Shonai 33 Kannon Pilgrimage (19th station) and the Dewa 13 Buddha Pilgrimage (7th station). It continues to play a role in local religious practices, such as the annual Omikoshi Festival on May 3rd, a remnant of its Shugendo heritage. == References == - Yuza Town History Editorial Committee, *Yuza Town Historical Materials No.1 (Mount Chokai Materials)*, 1977. - Yamagata Prefecture, *Yamagata Prefectural History, Vol. 15, Part 2*, 1979. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Warabioka Ennen*, Yuza Town Board of Education, 1994. - Yoriko Kanda (ed.), *Religious Folkloric Study of Warabioka Shugendo on Mount Chokai*, 1998. == External Links == {{commonscat|Ryuuzuji}}{{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|version=102560822|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11679435}} [[Category:Bettoji Temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Yusa Town]] [[Category:History of Yusa Town]] [[Category:Mount Chōkai]] [[Category:Temples of Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
